era_context: Two years before the formal establishment of the Principate, Octavian is consolidating his power following his victory over Mark Antony at the Battle of Actium in -31 BCE. The civil wars are ending, and Octavian is beginning to reorganize the state. In Egypt, Cleopatra VII is still alive but her power is waning; she will die in -30 BCE. In China, the Western Han dynasty continues under Emperor Xuan. The Parthian Empire is consolidating its power in the Near East.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior of the Americas: no written records

events.title: Octavian celebrates a triple triumph in Rome
events.description: Octavian celebrates a triple triumph in Rome, commemorating his victories over the Dalmatians, the Egyptians (under Cleopatra VII), and the Parthians (indirectly, through his ally Crassus). The triple triumph is a rare honor in Roman history, granted only to the greatest military commanders. The triumph is a massive propaganda event, designed to celebrate Octavian's achievements and to consolidate his power. The triumph includes processions, games, and public celebrations. This event marks the culmination of Octavian's military victories and the beginning of his transition from military commander to civilian ruler.
key_figures: Octavian (soon to be Augustus, Roman Emperor)
Cleopatra VII (Queen of Egypt, soon to be defeated)
events.certainty_note: Octavian's triple triumph is well-documented in multiple sources. The date of -29 BCE is confirmed by the Res Gestae and other sources.

events.title: Octavian closes the Temple of Janus Quirinus, symbolizing the end of the civil wars
events.description: Following his triple triumph, Octavian ceremonially closes the Temple of Janus Quirinus in the Roman Forum, a symbolic act indicating that Rome is at peace and no longer engaged in major military campaigns. This is only the second time in Roman history that the temple has been closed (the first being under Numa Pompilius in the legendary past). The closure is a powerful propaganda tool, reinforcing Octavian's image as a bringer of peace after decades of civil war. However, the closure is brief; the temple will be reopened within a few years as Rome continues military campaigns on its periphery.
events.certainty_note: The closure of the Temple of Janus is attested in multiple sources, but the exact dating is uncertain. Some sources place it in -29 BCE, others in -25 BCE. The Res Gestae mentions the closure but does not provide a specific date. Dio Cassius places it in -29 BCE.

events.title: Cleopatra VII prepares for her final confrontation with Octavian in Egypt
events.description: Cleopatra VII, Queen of Egypt, is preparing for her final confrontation with Octavian. Following the Battle of Actium in -31 BCE, where her ally Mark Antony was defeated, Cleopatra's position is increasingly precarious. Octavian is advancing toward Egypt, and Cleopatra is attempting to negotiate with him while also preparing for military resistance. She is also considering her options for escape or surrender. Within a year, Octavian will reach Egypt, and Cleopatra will choose to end her life rather than submit to Roman captivity.
key_figures: Mark Antony (Cleopatra's ally, defeated at Actium)
events.certainty_note: The general situation of Cleopatra in -29 BCE is well-established, but specific events are not uniquely documented. Plutarch and Dio Cassius provide accounts, but they are writing centuries later.

events.title: Emperor Xuan of Han continues his reign in China
events.description: Emperor Xuan of the Western Han dynasty (r. -74 to -49 BCE) continues his reign. He is consolidating his power by removing powerful consort families and eunuchs. The Xiongnu continue to pressure the northern borders. The Han economy is stable, but internal political instability remains a concern.
key_figures: Emperor Xuan of Han (r. -74 to -49 BCE)

sources.name: Han Shu (Book of Han) by Ban Gu

events.certainty_note: The general political situation is well-established, but specific events in -29 BCE are not uniquely documented.

events.title: Parthian Empire consolidates control over the Near East
events.description: The Parthian Empire continues to consolidate its control over Mesopotamia and the trade routes of the Near East. The Parthians are establishing themselves as a major power in the region, rivalling Rome.
key_figures: Orodes II (Parthian King, r. c. -57 to -37 BCE, though dating is uncertain)
events.certainty_note: Parthian chronology is uncertain. The general political situation is clear, but specific events in -29 BCE are not uniquely attested.

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of the closure of the Temple of Janus is disputed. Some scholars place it in -29 BCE, others in -25 BCE. The significance of the triple triumph is also questioned by some historians, who argue that it was primarily a propaganda event with limited real military significance. The narrative of Octavian's 'consolidation of power' is sometimes presented as inevitable, but modern scholarship emphasizes the continued instability and opposition he faced.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -29 BCE is moderate for the Roman world, with some contemporary and near-contemporary sources. However, these sources are heavily biased toward Octavian's perspective. The accounts of Cleopatra are particularly problematic, as they are written by Roman sources hostile to her. Chinese sources are similarly court-centric. The lives of ordinary people, women, slaves, and non-elite populations are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Octavian's triple triumph led to the closure of the Temple of Janus
Octavian's triumph is contemporary with Cleopatra's final preparations in Egypt
Cleopatra's situation in -29 BCE led to her death in -30 BCE
